title: Vomiting

infobox.value: Emesis, throwing up, puking, barfing, spewing, upchucking, heaving, hurling, ralphing, being sick, urping
Aspiration, electrolyte and water loss, damage to the enamel of the teeth, tear of the esophageal mucosa

History of migraine, history of PONV or motion sickness in a child's parent or sibling, better ASA physical status, intense preoperative anxiety, certain ethnicities or surgery types, decreased perioperative fluids, crystalloid versus colloid administration

(scientifically known as **emesis**, and otherwise known as **puking**, and **throwing up**)[[a]](#cite_note-1) is the forceful expulsion of the contents of one's [stomach](/wiki/Stomach "Stomach") through the [mouth](/wiki/Mouth "Mouth") and sometimes the [nose](/wiki/Nose "Nose").[[1]](#cite_note-2) The substance so produced (the erstwhile contents of the stomach, now expelled) is also known as **vomit** or **vomitus**.[[b]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Involuntary, forceful expulsion of stomach contents, typically via the mouth

Not to be confused with [Regurgitation (digestion)](/wiki/Regurgitation_(digestion) "Regurgitation (digestion)").

"Vomit", "Emesis", "Heaving", "Puke", and "Throw up" redirect here. For other uses, see [Vomit (disambiguation)](/wiki/Vomit_(disambiguation) "Vomit (disambiguation)"). For the butterfly genus, see [Emesis (genus)](/wiki/Emesis_(genus) "Emesis (genus)"). For the 2021 Argentine film, see [PussyCake](/wiki/PussyCake "PussyCake"). For the sailing terms, see [Heaving to](/wiki/Heaving_to "Heaving to") and [Careening](/wiki/Careening "Careening"). For the municipality of Albania, see [Pukë](/wiki/Puk%C3%AB "Pukë"). For the style of graffiti, see [Throw up (graffiti)](/wiki/Throw_up_(graffiti) "Throw up (graffiti)"). For other uses, see [Puke (disambiguation)](/wiki/Puke_(disambiguation) "Puke (disambiguation)").

**Vomiting** (scientifically known as **emesis**, and otherwise known as **puking**, and **throwing up**)[[a]](#cite_note-1) is the forceful expulsion of the contents of one's [stomach](/wiki/Stomach "Stomach") through the [mouth](/wiki/Mouth "Mouth") and sometimes the [nose](/wiki/Nose "Nose").[[1]](#cite_note-2) The substance so produced (the erstwhile contents of the stomach, now expelled) is also known as **vomit** or **vomitus**.[[b]](#cite_note-3)

Vomiting can be the result of ailments like [food poisoning](/wiki/Food-poisoning "Food-poisoning"), [gastroenteritis](/wiki/Gastroenteritis "Gastroenteritis"), [pregnancy](/wiki/Pregnancy "Pregnancy"), [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness"), or [hangover](/wiki/Hangover "Hangover"); or it can be an after effect of diseases such as [brain tumors](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor"), elevated [intracranial pressure](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure"), or overexposure to [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation").[[2]](#cite_note-hauser-4) The feeling that one is about to vomit is called [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"); it often precedes, but does not always lead to vomiting. Impairment due to [alcohol](/wiki/Alcoholic_drink "Alcoholic drink"), [anesthesia](/wiki/Anesthesia "Anesthesia"), or other [sedatives](/wiki/Sedatives "Sedatives") can cause inhalation of vomit. In severe cases, where [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration") develops, [intravenous](/wiki/Intravenous "Intravenous") fluid may be required. [Antiemetics](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") are sometimes necessary to suppress nausea and vomiting. Self-induced vomiting can be a component of an eating disorder such as [bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa"), and is itself now classified as an eating disorder on its own, [purging disorder](/wiki/Purging_disorder "Purging disorder").

## Complications

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Symptoms-vomiting.jpg/250px-Symptoms-vomiting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Symptoms-vomiting.jpg)

Vomiting

### Aspiration

Vomiting is dangerous if gastric content enters the [respiratory tract](/wiki/Respiratory_tract "Respiratory tract"). Under normal circumstances, the [gag reflex](/wiki/Gag_reflex "Gag reflex") and [coughing](/wiki/Coughing "Coughing") prevent this from occurring; however, these protective reflexes are compromised in persons who are under the influence of certain substances (including [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol")) or even mildly [anesthetized](/wiki/Anesthesia "Anesthesia"). The individual may choke and [asphyxiate](/wiki/Asphyxiation "Asphyxiation")[[3]](#cite_note-5) or develop [aspiration pneumonia](/wiki/Aspiration_pneumonia "Aspiration pneumonia").[[4]](#cite_note-6)

### Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance

Prolonged and excessive vomiting depletes the body of [water](/wiki/Water "Water") ([dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration")), and may alter the [electrolyte](/wiki/Electrolyte "Electrolyte") status. Gastric vomiting leads to the loss of acid and chloride directly. Combined with the resulting [alkaline tide](/wiki/Alkaline_tide "Alkaline tide"), this leads to [hypochloremic](/wiki/Hypochloremia "Hypochloremia") [metabolic alkalosis](/wiki/Metabolic_alkalosis "Metabolic alkalosis") (low [chloride](/wiki/Chloride "Chloride") levels together with high [HCO−  
3](/wiki/Bicarbonate "Bicarbonate") and CO  
2 and increased blood [pH](/wiki/PH "PH")) and often [hypokalemia](/wiki/Hypokalemia "Hypokalemia") ([potassium](/wiki/Potassium "Potassium") depletion). The hypokalemia is an indirect result of the [kidney](/wiki/Kidney "Kidney") compensating for the loss of acid. With the loss of intake of food the individual may eventually become [cachectic](/wiki/Cachexia "Cachexia"). A less frequent occurrence results from a vomiting of intestinal contents, including bile acids and HCO−  
3.

### Mallory–Weiss tear

See also: [Mallory–Weiss syndrome](/wiki/Mallory%E2%80%93Weiss_syndrome "Mallory–Weiss syndrome")

Repeated or profuse vomiting may cause erosions to the [esophagus](/wiki/Esophagus "Esophagus") or small tears in the esophageal mucosa ([Mallory–Weiss tear](/wiki/Mallory%E2%80%93Weiss_syndrome "Mallory–Weiss syndrome")). This may become apparent if fresh red blood is mixed with vomit after several episodes.

### Dentistry

Recurrent vomiting, such as observed in [bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa") or more rarely [anorexia nervosa](/wiki/Anorexia_nervosa "Anorexia nervosa"), may lead to the destruction of the [tooth enamel](/wiki/Tooth_enamel "Tooth enamel") due to the acidity of the vomit. [Digestive enzymes](/wiki/Digestive_enzyme "Digestive enzyme") can also have a negative effect on oral health, by degrading the tissue of the [gums](/wiki/Gums "Gums").

## Pathophysiology

Receptors on the floor of the [fourth ventricle](/wiki/Fourth_ventricle "Fourth ventricle") of the brain represent a [chemoreceptor trigger zone](/wiki/Chemoreceptor_trigger_zone "Chemoreceptor trigger zone"), known as the [area postrema](/wiki/Area_postrema "Area postrema"), stimulation of which can lead to vomiting.[[2]](#cite_note-hauser-4) The area postrema is a [circumventricular organ](/wiki/Circumventricular_organ "Circumventricular organ") and as such lies outside the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"); it can therefore be stimulated by blood-borne drugs that can [stimulate vomiting](/wiki/Emetic "Emetic") or [inhibit it](/wiki/Antiemetics "Antiemetics").[[5]](#cite_note-7)

There are various sources of input to the vomiting center:

* The chemoreceptor trigger zone at the base of the fourth ventricle has numerous [dopamine D2 receptors](/wiki/Dopamine_receptors "Dopamine receptors"), [serotonin 5-HT3 receptors](/wiki/5-HT_receptor "5-HT receptor"), [opioid receptors](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor"), [acetylcholine receptors](/wiki/Acetylcholine_receptor "Acetylcholine receptor"), and receptors for [substance P](/wiki/Substance_P "Substance P"). Stimulation of different receptors are involved in different pathways leading to emesis, in the final common pathway substance P appears involved.[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-pmid8129158-9)
* The [vestibular system](/wiki/Vestibular_system "Vestibular system"), which sends information to the brain via [cranial nerve VIII](/wiki/Cranial_nerve_VIII "Cranial nerve VIII") (vestibulocochlear nerve), plays a major role in [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness"), and is rich in [muscarinic receptors](/wiki/Muscarinic_acetylcholine_receptor "Muscarinic acetylcholine receptor") and [histamine H1 receptors](/wiki/Histamine_receptor "Histamine receptor").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid6399658-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)
* The [cranial nerve X](/wiki/Vagus_nerve "Vagus nerve") ([vagus nerve](/wiki/Vagus_nerve "Vagus nerve")) is activated when the [pharynx](/wiki/Pharynx "Pharynx") is irritated, leading to a [gag reflex](/wiki/Pharyngeal_reflex "Pharyngeal reflex").
* The vagal and [enteric nervous system](/wiki/Enteric_nervous_system "Enteric nervous system") inputs transmit information regarding the state of the [gastrointestinal system](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_system "Gastrointestinal system"). Irritation of the GI mucosa by [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy"), [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation"), distention, or acute infectious [gastroenteritis](/wiki/Gastroenteritis "Gastroenteritis") activates the 5-HT3 receptors of these inputs.
* The [CNS](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") mediates vomiting that arises from psychiatric disorders and stress from higher brain centers.[[10]](#cite_note-12)
* The [medulla](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata") plays an important role for triggering the vomiting act.[[11]](#cite_note-newrev2017-13)

The vomiting act encompasses three types of outputs initiated by the chemoreceptor trigger zone: Motor, [parasympathetic nervous system](/wiki/Parasympathetic_nervous_system "Parasympathetic nervous system") (PNS), and [sympathetic nervous system](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") (SNS). They are as follows:

* Increased [salivation](/wiki/Saliva "Saliva") to protect [tooth enamel](/wiki/Tooth_enamel "Tooth enamel") from stomach acids.[[12]](#cite_note-Mechanisms_of_nausea_and_vomiting-14) (Excessive vomiting leads to [dental erosion](/wiki/Erosion_(dental) "Erosion (dental)").) This is part of the PNS output.
* The body takes a deep breath to avoid [aspirating](/wiki/Pulmonary_aspiration "Pulmonary aspiration") vomit.[[12]](#cite_note-Mechanisms_of_nausea_and_vomiting-14)
* [Retroperistalsis](/wiki/Retroperistalsis "Retroperistalsis") starts from the middle of the [small intestine](/wiki/Small_intestine "Small intestine") and sweeps up digestive tract contents into the stomach, through the relaxed [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pylorus "Pylorus").[[12]](#cite_note-Mechanisms_of_nausea_and_vomiting-14)
* Intrathoracic pressure lowers (by [inspiration](/wiki/Inhalation "Inhalation") against a closed [glottis](/wiki/Glottis "Glottis")), coupled with an increase in abdominal pressure as the [abdominal muscles](/wiki/Abdomen "Abdomen") contract, propels stomach contents into the [esophagus](/wiki/Esophagus "Esophagus") as the [lower esophageal sphincter](/wiki/Cardia "Cardia") relaxes.[[13]](#cite_note-15) The stomach itself does not contract in the process of vomiting[[14]](#cite_note-16) except for at the [angular notch](/wiki/Angular_incisure "Angular incisure"), nor is there any retroperistalsis in the esophagus.
* Vomiting is ordinarily preceded by [retching](/wiki/Retching "Retching").[[12]](#cite_note-Mechanisms_of_nausea_and_vomiting-14)
* Vomiting also initiates an [SNS](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") response causing both sweating and increased heart rate.[[12]](#cite_note-Mechanisms_of_nausea_and_vomiting-14)

### Phases

The vomiting act has two phases. In the **retching phase**, the abdominal muscles undergo a few rounds of coordinated contractions together with the diaphragm and the muscles used in respiratory inspiration. For this reason, an individual may confuse this phase with an episode of violent [hiccups](/wiki/Hiccups "Hiccups"). In this retching phase, nothing has yet been expelled. In the next phase, also termed the **expulsive phase**, intense pressure is formed in the stomach brought about by enormous shifts in both the diaphragm and the abdomen. These shifts are, in essence, vigorous contractions of these muscles that last for extended periods of time—much longer than a normal period of muscular contraction. The pressure is then suddenly released when the upper esophageal sphincter relaxes resulting in the expulsion of gastric contents. As the mouth and [nasal cavity](/wiki/Nasal_cavity "Nasal cavity") are connected via the back of the throat, particularly forceful vomiting, or producing large quantities of vomit may result in material being ejected through the [nostrils](/wiki/Nostril "Nostril") in addition to the mouth. Individuals who do not regularly exercise their abdominal muscles may experience pain in those muscles for a few days. The decrease in pressure and the release of endorphins into the bloodstream after the expulsion causes the vomiter to feel relief almost immediately after vomiting.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Vomit_of_partially_digested_food_in_a_11_litre_container_Man-sized_glove_is_shown_in_photo_for_scale.jpg/250px-Vomit_of_partially_digested_food_in_a_11_litre_container_Man-sized_glove_is_shown_in_photo_for_scale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vomit_of_partially_digested_food_in_a_11_litre_container_Man-sized_glove_is_shown_in_photo_for_scale.jpg)

Partially digested food, with a [rubber glove](/wiki/Rubber_glove "Rubber glove") for scale

[Gastric secretions](/wiki/Gastric_acid "Gastric acid") and likewise vomit are highly [acidic](/wiki/Acid "Acid"). Recent food intake appears in the gastric vomit. Irrespective of the content, vomit tends to be [malodorous](/wiki/Odor "Odor").

The content of the vomitus (vomit) may be of medical interest. Fresh [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") in the vomit is termed [hematemesis](/wiki/Hematemesis "Hematemesis") ("blood vomiting"). Altered blood bears resemblance to coffee grounds (as the [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") in the blood is [oxidized](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation")) and, when this matter is identified, the term [coffee-ground vomiting](/wiki/Coffee_ground_vomiting "Coffee ground vomiting") is used. [Bile](/wiki/Bile "Bile") can enter the vomit during subsequent heaves due to [duodenal](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") contraction if the vomiting is severe. Fecal vomiting is often a consequence of [intestinal obstruction](/wiki/Intestinal_obstruction "Intestinal obstruction") or a gastrocolic [fistula](/wiki/Fistula "Fistula") and is treated as a warning sign of this potentially serious problem (*signum mali ominis*).

If the vomiting reflex continues for an extended period with no appreciable vomitus, the condition is known as non-productive emesis or "dry heaves", which can be painful and debilitating.

Color of vomit[[16]](#cite_note-18)

* Bright red in the vomit suggests bleeding from the esophagus
* Dark red vomit with liver-like clots suggests profuse bleeding in the stomach, such as from a perforated ulcer
* Coffee-ground-like vomit suggests less severe bleeding in the stomach because the gastric acid has had time to change the composition of the blood
* Yellow or green vomit suggests bile, indicating that the [pyloric valve](/wiki/Pyloric_valve "Pyloric valve") is open and bile is flowing into the stomach from the duodenum. This may occur during successive episodes of vomiting after the stomach contents have been completely expelled.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

## Causes

Vomiting may be due to a large number of causes, and [protracted](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/protracted "wikt:protracted") vomiting has a long [differential diagnosis](/wiki/Differential_diagnosis "Differential diagnosis").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Digestive tract

Causes in the [digestive tract](/wiki/Digestive_tract "Digestive tract")

* [Gastritis](/wiki/Gastritis "Gastritis") ([inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation") of the gastric wall)[[18]](#cite_note-20)
* [Gastroenteritis](/wiki/Gastroenteritis "Gastroenteritis")
* [Gastroesophageal reflux disease](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_reflux_disease "Gastroesophageal reflux disease")
* [Celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease")[[19]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-21)
* [Non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity")[[20]](#cite_note-VoltaCaio2017-22)
* [Pyloric stenosis](/wiki/Pyloric_stenosis "Pyloric stenosis") (in babies, this typically causes a very forceful "projectile vomiting" and is an indication for urgent surgery)
* [Bowel obstruction](/wiki/Bowel_obstruction "Bowel obstruction")
* [Overeating](/wiki/Overeating "Overeating") (stomach too full)
* [Acute abdomen](/wiki/Acute_abdomen "Acute abdomen") and/or [peritonitis](/wiki/Peritonitis "Peritonitis")
* [Ileus](/wiki/Ileus "Ileus")
* [Food allergies](/wiki/Food_allergies "Food allergies") (often in conjunction with [hives](/wiki/Hives "Hives") or [swelling](/wiki/Swelling_(medical) "Swelling (medical)"))
* [Cholecystitis](/wiki/Cholecystitis "Cholecystitis"), [pancreatitis](/wiki/Pancreatitis "Pancreatitis"), [appendicitis](/wiki/Appendicitis "Appendicitis"), [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis")
* [Food poisoning](/wiki/Food_poisoning "Food poisoning")
* In children, it can be caused by an [allergic reaction](/wiki/Allergic_reaction "Allergic reaction") to cow's [milk proteins](/wiki/Milk_proteins "Milk proteins") ([milk allergy](/wiki/Milk_allergy "Milk allergy") or [lactose intolerance](/wiki/Lactose_intolerance "Lactose intolerance"))

### Sensory system and brain

Causes in the [sensory system](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system"):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Movement leading to [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness") (which is caused by overstimulation of the labyrinthine [canals of the ear](/wiki/Ear_canal "Ear canal"))[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Ménière's disease](/wiki/M%C3%A9ni%C3%A8re%27s_disease "Ménière's disease")
* [Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo "Vertigo")

Causes in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Concussion](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion")
* [Cerebral hemorrhage](/wiki/Cerebral_hemorrhage "Cerebral hemorrhage")
* [Cerebral aneurysm](/wiki/Intracranial_aneurysm "Intracranial aneurysm")
* [Migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine")
* [Brain tumors](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor"), which can cause the chemoreceptors to malfunction
* [Benign intracranial hypertension](/wiki/Benign_intracranial_hypertension "Benign intracranial hypertension") and [hydrocephalus](/wiki/Hydrocephalus "Hydrocephalus")

[Metabolic](/wiki/Metabolic "Metabolic") disturbances (these may irritate both the stomach and the parts of the brain that coordinate vomiting):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Hypercalcemia](/wiki/Hypercalcemia "Hypercalcemia") (high [calcium](/wiki/Calcium_in_biology "Calcium in biology") levels)
* [Uremia](/wiki/Uremia "Uremia") ([urea](/wiki/Urea "Urea") accumulation, usually due to [kidney failure](/wiki/Kidney_failure "Kidney failure"))
* [Adrenal insufficiency](/wiki/Adrenal_insufficiency "Adrenal insufficiency")
* [Hypoglycemia](/wiki/Hypoglycemia "Hypoglycemia")
* [Hyperglycemia](/wiki/Hyperglycemia "Hyperglycemia")
* [Endometriosis](/wiki/Endometriosis "Endometriosis")[[21]](#cite_note-23)

[Pregnancy](/wiki/Pregnancy "Pregnancy"):[[22]](#cite_note-24)

* [Hyperemesis](/wiki/Hyperemesis_gravidarum "Hyperemesis gravidarum"), [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness")

[Drug reaction](/wiki/Drug_reaction "Drug reaction") (vomiting may occur as an acute [somatic](/wiki/Somatic_(biology) "Somatic (biology)") response to):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), which can be partially oxidized into [acetaldehyde](/wiki/Acetaldehyde "Acetaldehyde") that causes the symptoms of [hangover](/wiki/Hangover "Hangover"), including nausea, vomiting, shortness of breath, and fast heart rate.[[23]](#cite_note-25)
* [Opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid")
* [Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor")
* Many [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy") drugs
* Some [entheogens](/wiki/Entheogen "Entheogen") (such as [peyote](/wiki/Peyote "Peyote") or [ayahuasca](/wiki/Ayahuasca "Ayahuasca"))

[High altitude](/wiki/Effects_of_high_altitude_on_humans "Effects of high altitude on humans"):

* [Altitude sickness](/wiki/Altitude_sickness "Altitude sickness")[[24]](#cite_note-Fer2016-26)

[Illness](/wiki/Illness "Illness") (sometimes colloquially known as "[stomach flu](/wiki/Stomach_flu "Stomach flu")"—a broad name that refers to gastric inflammation caused by a range of viruses and bacteria):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Norovirus](/wiki/Norovirus "Norovirus") (formerly Norwalk virus or Norwalk agent)
* [Swine influenza](/wiki/Swine_influenza "Swine influenza")

Psychiatric/behavioral:

* [Bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa")
* [Food neophobia](/wiki/Food_neophobia "Food neophobia")
* [Purging disorder](/wiki/Purging_disorder "Purging disorder")

### Emetics

An *emetic*, such as [syrup of ipecac](/wiki/Syrup_of_ipecac "Syrup of ipecac"), is a substance that induces vomiting when administered orally or by injection. An emetic is used medically when a substance has been ingested and must be expelled from the body immediately. For this reason, many toxic and easily digestible products such as rat poison contain an emetic.[[25]](#cite_note-EB1911-27) This presents no problem for the effectiveness of the [rodenticide](/wiki/Rodenticide "Rodenticide") as rodents are unable to vomit.[[26]](#cite_note-Kapoor-et-al-2015-28) Inducing vomiting can remove the substance before it is absorbed into the body.
Emetics can be divided into two categories: those which produce their effect by acting on the vomiting center in the medulla, and those which act directly on the stomach itself. Some emetics, such as ipecac, fall into both categories: they initially act directly on the stomach, while their further and more vigorous effect occurs by stimulation of the medullary center.[[25]](#cite_note-EB1911-27)

[Salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt") water and [mustard](/wiki/Mustard_seed "Mustard seed") water, which act directly on the stomach, have been used since ancient times as emetics.[[27]](#cite_note-29) Care must be taken with salt, as [excessive intake can potentially be harmful](/wiki/Hypernatremia "Hypernatremia").[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)
[Copper sulfate](/wiki/Copper(II)_sulfate "Copper(II) sulfate") was also used in the past as an emetic.[[30]](#cite_note-pmid4385403-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) It is now considered too toxic for this use.[[32]](#cite_note-isbn0-8385-8172-2-34)

[Hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide") is used as an emetic in veterinary practice.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

### Self-induced

* [Eating disorders](/wiki/Eating_disorder "Eating disorder") ([anorexia nervosa](/wiki/Anorexia_nervosa "Anorexia nervosa") or [bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa"))
* To eliminate an ingested poison (some poisons should not be vomited as they may be more toxic when inhaled or aspirated; it is better to ask for help before inducing vomiting)
* Some people who engage in [binge drinking](/wiki/Binge_drinking "Binge drinking") induce vomiting to make room in their stomachs for more alcohol consumption.
* Participants in [milk chugging](/wiki/Milk_chugging "Milk chugging") typically end up vomiting most of the milk they consume, as proteins in the ingested milk (such as [casein](/wiki/Casein "Casein")) rapidly denature and unravel on contact with gastric acid and [protease](/wiki/Protease "Protease") enzymes, rapidly filling the stomach. Once the stomach becomes full, [stretch receptors](/wiki/Stretch_receptor "Stretch receptor") in the stomach wall trigger signals to vomit to expel any further liquid the participant ingests.
* People suffering from [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea") may induce vomiting in hopes of feeling better.

### Miscellaneous

* After [surgery](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery") ([postoperative nausea and vomiting](/wiki/Postoperative_nausea_and_vomiting "Postoperative nausea and vomiting"))
* Disagreeable sights or [disgust](/wiki/Disgust "Disgust"), smells, tastes, sounds or thoughts (such as decayed matter, others' vomit, thinking of vomiting), etc.
* Extreme pain, such as an intense [headache](/wiki/Headache "Headache") or [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") (heart attack)
* Extreme [emotions](/wiki/Emotions "Emotions")
* [Cyclic vomiting syndrome](/wiki/Cyclic_vomiting_syndrome "Cyclic vomiting syndrome") (a poorly understood condition with attacks of vomiting)
* [Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome](/wiki/Cannabinoid_hyperemesis_syndrome "Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome") (similar to [cyclic vomiting syndrome](/wiki/Cyclic_vomiting_syndrome "Cyclic vomiting syndrome"), but has cannabis use as its underlying cause).
* High doses of [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") sometimes trigger a vomit reflex.
* Violent fits of [coughing](/wiki/Coughing "Coughing"), [hiccups](/wiki/Hiccups "Hiccups"), or [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma")
* [Anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety")
* [Depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)")
* [Overexertion](/wiki/Overexertion "Overexertion") (doing too much strenuous exercise can lead to vomiting shortly afterwards).

### Other types

* *Projectile vomiting* is vomiting that ejects the gastric contents with great force.[[35]](#cite_note-med_dict-37) It is a classic symptom of [infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis](/wiki/Pyloric_stenosis "Pyloric stenosis"), in which it typically follows feeding and can be so forceful that some material exits through the nose.[[36]](#cite_note-Sleisenger783-38)

## Treatment

An [antiemetic](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") is a [drug](/wiki/Medication "Medication") that is effective against vomiting and [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"). Antiemetics are typically used to treat [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness") and the [side effects](/wiki/Adverse_effect_(medicine) "Adverse effect (medicine)") of medications such as [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") and [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy").

Antiemetics act by inhibiting the receptor sites associated with emesis. Hence, anticholinergics, antihistamines, dopamine antagonists, serotonin antagonists, and cannabinoids are used as antiemetics.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Evidence to support the use of [antiemetics](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") for nausea and vomiting among adults in the [emergency department](/wiki/Emergency_department "Emergency department") is poor.[[38]](#cite_note-Fur2015-40) It is unclear if any medication is better than another or better than no active treatment.[[38]](#cite_note-Fur2015-40)

## Epidemiology

[Nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea") and/or vomiting are the main complaints in 1.6% of visits to family physicians in Australia.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

## Society and culture

[Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus"), writing on the culture of the [ancient Persians](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") and highlighting the differences with those of the [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), notes that to vomit in the presence of others is prohibited among Persians.[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Nationalmuseet_-_Cophenaghen_-_brygos_vomiting1.jpg/250px-Nationalmuseet_-_Cophenaghen_-_brygos_vomiting1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nationalmuseet_-_Cophenaghen_-_brygos_vomiting1.jpg)

A drunk man vomiting, while a young slave is holding his forehead. [Brygos Painter](/wiki/Brygos_Painter "Brygos Painter"), 500–470 BC

It is quite common that, when one person vomits, others nearby become nauseated, particularly when smelling the vomit of others, and often to the point of vomiting themselves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This tendency in human populations has been observed at drinking parties, where excessive consumption of [alcoholic beverages](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage") may cause a number of party members to vomit nearly simultaneously, triggered by the initial vomiting of a single member of the party.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Intense vomiting in [ayahuasca](/wiki/Ayahuasca "Ayahuasca") [ceremonies](/wiki/Ceremony "Ceremony") is a common phenomenon. However, people who experience "la purga" after drinking ayahuasca, in general, regard the practise as both a physical and spiritual cleanse and often come to welcome it.[[42]](#cite_note-44) It has been suggested that the consistent emetic effects of ayahuasca—in addition to its many other therapeutic properties—was of medicinal benefit to [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people") of the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_Basin "Amazon Basin"), in helping to clear [parasites](/wiki/Parasite "Parasite") from the gastrointestinal system.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

There have also been documented cases of a single ill and vomiting individual inadvertently causing others to vomit, when they are especially fearful of also becoming ill, through a form of [mass hysteria](/wiki/Mass_hysteria "Mass hysteria").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Sea-sickness_bag%2C_Merseyside_Maritime_Museum.png/250px-Sea-sickness_bag%2C_Merseyside_Maritime_Museum.png)](/wiki/File:Sea-sickness_bag,_Merseyside_Maritime_Museum.png)

Special bags are often supplied on boats for sick passengers to vomit into.

Most people try to contain their vomit by vomiting into a sink, toilet, or trash can, as vomit is difficult and unpleasant to clean. On airplanes and boats, [special bags](/wiki/Sickness_bag "Sickness bag") are supplied for sick passengers to vomit into. A special disposable bag (leakproof, puncture-resistant, odorless) containing absorbent material that solidifies the vomit quickly is also available, making it convenient and safe to store until there is an opportunity to dispose of it conveniently.

People who vomit chronically (e.g., as part of an [eating disorder](/wiki/Eating_disorder "Eating disorder") such as [bulimia nervosa](/wiki/Bulimia_nervosa "Bulimia nervosa")) may devise various ways to hide this disorder.

An online study of people's responses to "horrible sounds" found vomiting "the most disgusting". Professor Trevor Cox of the [University of Salford](/wiki/University_of_Salford "University of Salford")'s Acoustic Research Centre said, "We are pre-programmed to be repulsed by horrible things such as vomiting, as it is fundamental to staying alive to avoid nasty stuff." It is thought that [disgust](/wiki/Disgust "Disgust") is triggered by the sound of vomiting to protect those nearby from possibly diseased food.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Psychology

[Emetophilia](/wiki/Emetophilia "Emetophilia") is sexual arousal from vomiting, or watching others vomit.[[45]](#cite_note-47) [Emetophobia](/wiki/Emetophobia "Emetophobia") is a phobia that causes overwhelming, intense anxiety pertaining to vomiting.
